author: @B2Hives
text:
1975 clinical setting, we used 10 parts bleach with 90 parts water in spray bottles to disinfect areas.  1978,  lab tests, cultures and sensitivities showed that a  1 to 10 parts was effective to kill all but one bacteria and virus.  The Norovirus was not killed.  
Looking at the value,  1 liter of Bleach making 10L of spray,  it will go a long way, and last a long time,  It is best to mix in small amounts as you use them.
As a curiosity,  I found one of the new make it in the spray pump bottles with USB power for 7usd.  
The first cycle, I would suspect by the Nose Sniff evaluation, it was almost at the 1% level,  Pluged in and did cycle 2, then 3, and 4.  each time the concentration by sniff test was more concentrated.

author: @Scott_C
text: Now...  This is one of those why aren't more people doing this across the world?  You hear stories of people lacking clean water.  Gather some charcoal from a fire mash it up fine with some sand,  run the water through a large bucket of it:  Large particles removed.  Get some salt water from the ocean or makes some.  Do this with a few batteries, charcoal sticks would work as well as the rods,  batteries and 6V generation are dead simple.  Use the bleach, lightly, to sanitise gallons of the previously filtered water.

author: @phbrinsden
text: This is exactly how I sanitize my “salt” water pool.   Salt level is about same as in your tears so very easy on eyes when swimming.   When the circulation pump runs it turns on the low DC voltage in the cell just after the filter and voila, gently sanitized pool water.   As the sun burns off the chlorine the pH rises and you just add muriatic acid (HCl) to lower the pH again.   The acid replaces the lost chlorine, combines with the sodium that was left and you have salt again.    Great system and so easy and safe to use versus chlorine powders etc.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
I've always wondered why it's recommended to try to lower/balance the pH, when higher pH tends to be very anti-microbial itself?   For example, when one wants to grow their own spirulina--you get your pH up to around 9.5 to 10 pH or so, because very few organisms besides spirulina will actually grow in it.  

  Just something I've wondered about.  Sometimes I wonder if it's more about getting you to buy more chemicals.

author: @TacticalFlatcap
text: 12-18v solar panels are readily available cheaply for trickle charging car batteries and the like, so thinking an off grid solution flushing either water and salt as you have it or else seawater.  if the voltage was increased, what effect would that have on the end product concentration and what would make the end solution a dangerous concentration (what not to do?)

replies.author: @werxeh8027
replies.text:
There are videos on YouTube of people doing this using car batteries, and collecting the gasses in bottles over the electrodes as well. So long as the electrodes are close to each the amount of chlorine gas released shouldn't be too high. That said, with proximity the current would be higher, so perhaps the best option for a car battery setup would be something like a closed funnel above both electrodes to bubble any gas to the sides after having given it enough time to react with anything in the electrolyte first. The maximum possible concentration is down to the concentration of salt you initially put into the water though. I'd say it's practically impossible to produce anything stronger than your average household thin bleach, but I could be wrong. If you keep it below 2g of salt per 100ml though I don't think it is possible to produce anything more dangerous than that. After all, there's a finite amount of chlorine in the solution.

Incidentally you can pick up a 100W solar panel for about £75 if you already have a charge controller, no need to trickle charge. I'm running a CCTV camera and a raspberry pi doubling as a recording device for the camera and a home media server on one of those panels.

author: @benthere8051
text:
I used to collect the carbon rods from carbon/zinc batteries for electrical and electrolysis experiments as a child, some 65 years ago. None of the texts that I read ever mentioned paraffin. I'll bet my experiments would have performed much better had I known to remove the wax coating,  lol. How can we test our disinfectant to see if it will be effective? If it passes the smell test can we assume it's good enough?
Thanks again, Robert. I've been enjoying your experiments.

I read recently that a potassium permanganate solution was good for disinfecting contaminated vegetables, for treating drinking water, and for treating fungal infections. It is really cheap and is on the WHO list as a "must-have" chemical for emergency kits.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: If one takes carbon rods and connected to A 12v lead acid battery and  shorts the rods together, they will generate enough heat to burn off the waxes.

@werxeh8027

replies.author: @werxeh8027
replies.text: @bblrip3690 the more you know :) It doesn't make much sense to use it as a hand sanitiser really given the cost and much cheaper alternatives. It's good for wound dressings though. On a somewhat related note, the lack of brass door handles these days will also increase the spread of bacteria and viruses, brass and copper naturally destroy them quite quickly because of their reactivity (that smell we associate with metal after touching reactive metals like copper and brass is actually caused by it reacting with the oils on our skin).

replies.author: @bblrip3690
@werxeh8027
replies.text: @bblrip3690 ah, well that's good, but I would personally keep it for wound dressings. Basic bar soap is very good at killing bacteria and viruses on the skin because of the fact one end of the molecule is hydrophilic and the other hydrophobic, it essentially pulls any small organisms apart. Sodium hypochlorite is also a very safe surface disinfectant, and really cheap to buy, but as this video demonstrates, also really easy to make. If you live anywhere near seawater you don't even need to buy salt.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: I suspect they are using platinum coated plates for longevity of the electrodes. And though they are likely making a profit, it probably isn't as much as a comparison against graphite (which will need replacing) would suggest.  But, when we have these things laying around, say a 5v usb charger and some pencils, then one might not need to go buy an expensive electrolisys unit.

author: @baraBober
text: And I thought I was making alkaline/hydrogen water 0_o... speaking of which, if you mix a bit of baking soda with water, the PH level raises to about 9, however if you apply electrical current to it, PH goes to 11. Any idea why?

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: Sodium bicarbonate is a salt of carbon dioxide and sodium hydroxide.  I suspect that some of the CO2 evolved out of solution  leaving sodium hydroxide. If you let it sit long enough, it will take co2 from the atmosphere and the PH will drop back down.

replies.author: @werxeh8027
replies.text:
@kreynolds1123 actually the reaction of electrolysis of sodium bicarbonate solution shouldn't affect the sodium bicarbonate at all, it ought to remain unchanged whilst facilitating the release of hydrogen and oxygen gases. Any change in pH should only occur as a result of the reduction in the volume of water, resulting in a more saturated sodium bicarbonate solution (and possibly only during electrolysis as part of an intermediary reaction)

EDIT: After doing some cursory reading, it may be possible at very high temperatures or high voltages that some CO2 be released, which could result in some small amount of sodium hydroxide being produced.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @werxeh8027 a little further researching and one can find That sodium carbonate has a ph of  11.6, about the same as the original poster PH of 11. In a single cell it's probable that if some co2 escapes, the sodium hydroxide will react with sodium bicarbonate forming sodium carbonate.

replies.author: @werxeh8027
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 that makes sense :) Also a good thing, because sodium hydroxide even in small amounts can be pretty hazardous. Now I think about it, seems pretty obviously you'd end up with sodium carbonate first from sodium bicarbonate if you lose some of the carbon. But at low temperatures and normal voltages, I don't think that ought to happen at all. If they were testing the pH during the electrolysis and not afterwards, it's possible they're picking up on intermediary compounds still.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123

author: @werxeh8027
text:
I have one that I paid £7 for, little USB thing you put in any container. I know some are being sold as part of a spray bottle. I removed the original USB connector because I wanted to make larger amounts and it defaulted to turning off every 3 minutes without being reconnected and it's designed to make 100ml with 2g of salt. 

I was going to suggest the carbon rods from AA or AAA batteries but you beat me to it :) Also carbon felt someone might have from trying to make a zinc bromine battery works too. You can also use an old computer PSU as the power source, 3.3V, 5V and 12V outputs, just short the green cable on the connector that plugs into the motherboard to one of the neutral black cables to bypass the need for a power switch. Also any remotely modern PSU will turn itself off in case of a short so it's pretty safe

replies.author: @werxeh8027
replies.text: Also you can make 200ppm free chlorine quite easily with either set up. I got some chlorine testing strips to get an idea of the time and amount of salt needed based on the amount of water I used. Can use it to bleach dye out of fabrics. It is best to have it near an open window if you're making larger quantities because some chlorine gas is released of course, and if you use higher voltages that'll happen more quickly. Also best to have the electrodes as close together as possible, so the chlorine gas has the most chance to react with the sodium hydroxide produced at the other electrode (which is why you get sodium hypochlorite of course).
